---
title: "Why I Switched to Polars From Pandas"
subtitle: "Benchmarking Polars vs Pandas DataFrames"
date: "2025-10-21"
description: >
  If you use Pandas DataFrames to store and analyze large datasets, consider switching to Polars.
  Benchmarks across read, write, construct, pivot, join, and filter operations — Polars wins every time.
categories: [Python, Polars, Pandas, Performance]
---

# TL;DR

If you use **Pandas** DataFrames to store and analyze large datasets, consider making a change 
to **Polars**.

DataFrames allow me to think relationally — just like SQL — and I use them extensively in virtually 
all of my work: app development, exploratory data analysis, and summarizing large datasets.

::: {.callout-note}
**System & Environment**

All benchmarks ran on a **16-inch MacBook Pro (2021)** with an **Apple M1 Max** chip and **64 GB** 
of unified memory, running macOS Sonoma 14.7.

Tests used **Python 3.10.9**, **Pandas 1.5.3** and **Polars 1.33.0**.
:::

**Methodology**: Each operation was executed **1,000 times** per library, and the **mean execution 
time** was recorded.

---

# Read | Write

```{python}
#| eval: false
# Download 10 MB test file
# https://raw.githubusercontent.com/frictionlessdata/datasets/main/files/csv/10mb.csv

import pandas as pd
import polars as pl
import timeit

FILE = "10mb.csv"

pd_read_time = timeit.timeit(lambda: pd.read_csv(FILE), number=1000)
pl_read_time = timeit.timeit(lambda: pl.read_csv(FILE), number=1000)

print(f"Pandas mean read:  {pd_read_time / 1000:.4f}s")
print(f"Polars mean read:  {pl_read_time / 1000:.4f}s")
print(f"Speed increase:    {pd_read_time / pl_read_time:.2f}x")
```

| Metric | Mean Speed Increase | Standard Deviation |
|---|---|---|
| Read | 16.79x | 3.57 |
| Write | 30.74x | 5.32 |

Polars is faster at reading and writing CSVs because it's built in Rust with parallelized, 
memory-efficient algorithms that fully utilize multiple CPU cores.

---

# Construct | Pivot

## Simulate Dataset

```{python}
#| eval: false
import numpy as np
import string
import random

N = int(5e6)


def random_str(n=5):
    return "".join(random.choices(string.ascii_letters, k=n))


data = {
    "id": np.arange(N),
    "value": np.random.randint(0, 1000, size=N),
    "flag": np.random.choice([True, False], size=N),
    "category": np.random.choice(["A", "B", "C", "D"], size=N),
    "score": np.random.randn(N),
    "year": np.random.randint(2000, 2025, size=N),
    "name": [random_str(5) for _ in range(N)],
    "ratio": np.random.random(size=N),
    "count": np.random.randint(1, 100, size=N),
}
```

| Metric | Mean Speed Increase | Standard Deviation |
|---|---|---|
| Constructor | 3.41x | 0.43 |
| Group By | 10.18x | 1.58 |

**Constructor**: Polars uses Rust's zero-copy memory model and parallel data parsing, avoiding 
Python's object overhead.

**Pivot Tables**: Polars executes grouped aggregations in parallel and leverages an optimized 
query engine built on Apache Arrow.

---

# Joins | Filter

| Metric | Mean Speed Increase | Standard Deviation |
|---|---|---|
| Inner Join | 1.71x | 1.19 |
| Union All | 8.91x | 16.02 |
| Filter | 2.38x | 1.46 |

**Joins**: Polars uses parallelized hash join algorithms implemented in Rust, operating directly 
on Arrow memory and minimizing copying.

**Filter**: Polars evaluates filter expressions lazily and in parallel at the columnar level, 
enabling vectorized, cache-efficient filtering.

> **Note on Union All variance**: Polars' `vstack()` uses multi-threaded Arrow buffer operations 
> that improve mean runtime but introduce non-deterministic variance — the OS schedules threads 
> differently across performance and efficiency cores.

---

# Conclusion

Across the board, `Polars` is faster at all typical DataFrame operations tested. The syntax 
differences are minimal and AI can quickly convert your `Pandas` code.

If you work with large DataFrames, Polars is worth the 30-minute migration: faster runtimes, 
smaller memory footprint, and minimal code changes.

---

# Code Reference

| Operation | Pandas | Polars | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Read CSV** | `pd.read_csv("data.csv")` | `pl.read_csv("data.csv")` | 16.79x |
| **Write CSV** | `df.to_csv("out.csv")` | `df.write_csv("out.csv")` | 30.74x |
| **Constructor** | `pd.DataFrame(data)` | `pl.DataFrame(data)` | 3.41x |
| **Group By** | `df.groupby("col")["value"].mean()` | `df.groupby("col").agg(pl.col("value").mean())` | 10.18x |
| **Inner Join** | `df1.merge(df2, on="id", how="inner")` | `df1.join(df2, on="id", how="inner")` | 1.71x |
| **Union All** | `pd.concat([df1, df2], ignore_index=True)` | `df1.vstack(df2)` | 8.91x |
| **Filter** | `df[df["value"] > 10]` | `df.filter(pl.col("value") > 10)` | 2.38x |

\*Results will vary depending on machine and dataset size/structure.
